turns.content: I'm trying to port a C++ library that uses heavy template metaprogramming, but I'm worried Rust generics will make my binary huge because every type variant gets its own code. Is there a way to keep the binary small while still using generics?

That's a common concern when moving from C++ templates, but Rust handles this differently than you might expect. When you write generic functions in Rust like impl<T> for your data structure, the compiler performs monomorphization at compile time, generating specific code only for each concrete type it encounters during linking. This means if you don't explicitly instantiate a specific type, that code simply isn't included in the final binary.
So I can just avoid instantiating certain types and they won't bloat my executable? Can I tell the compiler to skip generating code for some generic functions without using traits?

Exactly, you are right about skipping instantiation. Rust provides features like #[cfg] attributes that allow conditional compilation based on whether a type is used in your crate or not. For example, if you have impl<T> for your generic struct but only use it with i32 and f64 in the main binary, those other types simply won't be compiled into the final output.
That sounds promising. But what if I need to support many different types? Will Rust still generate a lot of code for all of them?

You are correct that widespread usage will increase the binary size, but there is a better alternative when you truly need dynamic behavior. If your C++ library relies on runtime type checks or polymorphism through virtual functions, Rust can offer a similar solution using traits and dynamic dispatch with trait objects like &dyn Trait instead of generic types. While this trades some performance for flexibility, it prevents the monomorphization explosion entirely.

Ah-ha! So if I replace my generic structs with trait objects and use dyn Trait, I avoid the compile-time code generation altogether? That would be a huge relief for porting this library without breaking compatibility.

Precisely. By switching to dynamic dispatch via traits like impl Trait + std::any::Any, you trade the compile-time overhead of monomorphization for runtime flexibility. You can verify this trade-off yourself using cargo bloat or rustc --print target-specs to measure how much code is actually being generated versus what you expect.
